name: The Hero's Journey
description: Joseph Campbell's classic monomyth, in three acts and twelve stages.

acts.name: Act 1 - Departure
acts.guidance: The hero's ordinary life, the call that disrupts it, and the choice to cross into the unknown. What does your protagonist's world look like before everything changes, and what pulls them out of it?

acts.name: Act 2 - Descent and Initiation
acts.guidance: The hero is tested in an unfamiliar world by allies, enemies, and a central ordeal that forces them to change. What trials do they face, and what do they nearly lose?

acts.name: Act 3 - Return and Reintegration
acts.guidance: The hero carries what they've won back to where they started, transformed by the journey. How do they return, and what do they bring back with them?

name: Three-Act Structure
description: The classic setup, confrontation, and resolution structure.

acts.name: Act 1 - Setup
acts.guidance: Introduce your protagonist, their world, and the event that kicks the story into motion. What's normal life, and what breaks it?

acts.name: Act 2 - Confrontation
acts.guidance: Your protagonist pursues their goal against mounting obstacles, hitting a midpoint that raises the stakes. What stands in their way, and how do things get worse before they get better?

acts.name: Act 3 - Resolution
acts.guidance: The conflict comes to a head and resolves. How does the story's central question get answered, and where do your characters land?

name: Save the Cat Beat Sheet
description: Blake Snyder's fifteen-beat structure for tightly plotted stories.

acts.name: Act 1
acts.guidance: Establish your hero's flawed status quo, state the story's theme, and hit them with a catalyst that breaks into a new world. What do they believe at the start, and what event forces a decision?

acts.name: Act 2
acts.guidance: Your hero explores the new world, develops a B story, and hits a midpoint before things fall apart completely. What do they gain, then lose, and what's their darkest moment?

acts.name: Act 3
acts.guidance: Armed with what they've learned, your hero executes a plan to resolve the A and B stories. How do they prove they've changed, and how does it all wrap up?

name: Freytag's Pyramid
description: Gustav Freytag's five-part dramatic arc, rising to a climax and falling to resolution.

acts.name: Exposition
acts.guidance: Establish the world, characters, and status quo before the story's central conflict ignites. What do we need to know, and what event disrupts it?

acts.name: Rising Action
acts.guidance: A sequence of complications that build tension and raise the stakes toward the climax. What obstacles pile up, and how does the pressure mount?

acts.name: Climax
acts.guidance: The turning point — the moment of highest tension, where the outcome hangs in the balance. What's the decisive confrontation or choice?

acts.name: Falling Action
acts.guidance: The immediate consequences of the climax begin to unfold. What's the fallout, and how do things start to settle?

acts.name: Denouement
acts.guidance: Loose ends resolve and the story reaches its new equilibrium. Where do your characters end up?

name: Dan Harmon's Story Circle
description: An eight-step descent into the unfamiliar and return, adapted from the hero's journey.

acts.name: Descent
acts.guidance: Your character leaves their comfort zone in pursuit of a need, searching an unfamiliar situation. What do they want, and what do they find when they go looking?

acts.name: Ascent
acts.guidance: Having paid a price, your character takes what they found and returns home changed. What do they take, and how are they different for it?

name: Romance Beat Sheet
description: A genre-focused structure built around the central relationship, from first meeting to happily-ever-after.

acts.name: Act 1 - Meeting
acts.guidance: Establish both leads in their separate worlds, bring them together, and plant the spark and the obstacle that keeps them apart. Who are they before each other, and what draws them together despite the odds?

acts.name: Act 2 - Falling
acts.guidance: The relationship deepens as the leads are pushed together, hit a point where turning back is no longer an option, and bond over a shared vulnerability at the midpoint. What draws them closer, and what do they risk by getting close?

acts.name: Act 3 - Breaking and Choosing
acts.guidance: External or internal pressure drives the leads apart at the black moment, before one makes a grand gesture to prove they've changed. What breaks them apart, and what does it take to win each other back?
chapters: Happily Ever After / Happy For Now

name: Story Clock
description: A twelve-beat structure mapped to the four quarters of a clock face.

acts.name: 12:00 to 3:00 - Setup
acts.guidance: Establish the ordinary world, introduce the cast, and trigger the inciting incident. Who are we following, and what sets things in motion?

acts.name: 3:00 to 6:00 - Rising Action
acts.guidance: Your protagonist commits to the new situation and meets their first real obstacles. What changes once they're in motion, and what pushes back?

acts.name: 6:00 to 9:00 - Complications
acts.guidance: The midpoint shifts the story's direction, stakes rise, and a major setback tests your protagonist. What goes wrong, and how does it change the stakes?

acts.name: 9:00 to 12:00 - Resolution
acts.guidance: The story turns toward its climax and resolves. How does everything come to a head, and what's left once the dust settles?

name: Seven-Point Story Structure
description: Dan Wells' backward-plotted structure built around two pinch points and a midpoint shift.

acts.name: Act 1 - Setup
acts.guidance: Open with a hook that shows your protagonist's starting state, then hit a plot turn that hands them a goal. Where do they begin, and what launches them toward it?

acts.name: Act 2 - Rising Pressure
acts.guidance: Pressure from the antagonist forces your protagonist to react, then a midpoint flips them from reactive to proactive. What squeezes them first, and what changes their approach?

acts.name: Act 3 - Escalation and Resolution
acts.guidance: A second, harder squeeze strips away options before a final turn sends your protagonist into the resolution. What's the worst it gets, and how do they finally act to end it?

name: Mystery Beat Sheet
description: A genre-focused structure for whodunits and detective stories, from the crime to the reveal.

acts.name: Act 1 - The Crime
acts.guidance: Establish your detective and their world, then drop the crime that pulls them in. Who's investigating, and what's the puzzle that hooks them?

acts.name: Act 2 - The Investigation
acts.guidance: Clues surface alongside red herrings that mislead both detective and reader, building toward a midpoint discovery that reframes the case. What false trails get laid, and what breaks the case open?

acts.name: Act 3 - The Reveal
acts.guidance: The detective connects the final pieces, confronts the culprit, and lays out the solution. What's the last clue that cracks it, and how does the truth come out?

name: Forced Proximity Romance
description: A romance built on circumstance throwing two uninterested leads together until they can't stay apart.

acts.name: Act 1 - Thrown Together
acts.guidance: Establish both leads as content apart, then have circumstance pair them up against their will. Who are they before this, and what forces them into each other's orbit?

acts.name: Act 2 - Close Quarters
acts.guidance: Proximity chips away at their resistance as they're forced to rely on each other, hitting a midpoint where indifference cracks into real feeling. What breaks the ice, and what moment makes the attraction undeniable?

acts.name: Act 3 - Choosing Each Other
acts.guidance: The circumstance that threw them together ends or threatens to, forcing a real choice about what they want. What pulls them apart when proximity is no longer forcing them together, and what makes them choose to stay?

name: Heist Structure
description: A caper structure built around assembling a crew and executing a plan that inevitably goes sideways.

acts.name: Act 1 - The Job
acts.guidance: Introduce the target and the reason it's worth the risk, then assemble the crew and their plan. What's the prize, and who's pulled together to go after it?

acts.name: Act 2 - The Execution
acts.guidance: The crew sets the plan in motion, hits a midpoint complication that forces improvisation, and discovers a betrayal or hidden angle. What goes wrong first, and who isn't who they seemed?

acts.name: Act 3 - The Score
acts.guidance: Everything converges on the job itself, revealing how much of the plan was misdirection all along. How does the crew pull it off, and what's the final twist on who gets what?

name: Rescue Mission
description: An action structure built around getting someone back: infiltration, extraction, and the getaway.

acts.name: Act 1 - The Taking
acts.guidance: Establish who's been taken and why it matters, then show the protagonist committing to get them back. Who's lost, and what does it cost the protagonist to go after them?

acts.name: Act 2 - Getting In
acts.guidance: The protagonist gathers what they need and infiltrates enemy territory, hitting a midpoint discovery that changes the mission. What do they need to get in, and what do they learn once they're inside?

acts.name: Act 3 - Getting Out
acts.guidance: The extraction goes wrong, forcing a fight or chase to get everyone out alive. What breaks the plan, and how do they make it out?

name: Horror / Survival
description: A dread-building structure that escalates from an uneasy calm to a fight for survival.

acts.name: Act 1 - The Calm Before
acts.guidance: Establish a normal, even idyllic, world and plant the first wrong note that something isn't right. What does safety look like here, and what small detail hints it won't last?

acts.name: Act 2 - Escalating Dread
acts.guidance: The threat reveals itself in earnest, isolating your characters and picking them off as they scramble for answers. What cuts off their escape routes, and what do they learn about the thing hunting them?

acts.name: Act 3 - The Reckoning
acts.guidance: Survivors are forced into a final confrontation with the threat, on its terms or theirs. Who's left, and what does it take to survive - or does anyone?

name: Coming of Age
description: An internal growth arc following a protagonist from innocence through a disillusioning event to hard-won maturity.

acts.name: Act 1 - Innocence
acts.guidance: Establish your protagonist's sheltered or naive understanding of the world, and the person or event that first cracks it. What do they believe before they know better, and what shakes that belief?

acts.name: Act 2 - Disillusionment
acts.guidance: Your protagonist confronts the gap between what they believed and how the world actually works, stumbling through mistakes as old certainties fail. What do they get wrong before they start getting it right, and what illusion finally breaks at the midpoint?

acts.name: Act 3 - Maturity
acts.guidance: Your protagonist faces a defining choice that requires the person they've become, not the one they were. What does this choice cost them, and who are they on the other side of it?

name: Espionage Thriller
description: A spy structure built on divided loyalties, where the danger comes from within the mission itself.

acts.name: Act 1 - The Mission
acts.guidance: Establish your operative and the mission they're recruited or assigned to, along with the people they'll have to trust. Who are they working for, and who can they actually rely on?

acts.name: Act 2 - Divided Loyalties
acts.guidance: Trust erodes as the operative gathers intelligence and starts to suspect someone close to the mission isn't what they claim. What do they uncover, and who do they start to doubt at the midpoint?

acts.name: Act 3 - The Reveal
acts.guidance: The mole or double-cross is exposed, forcing a final gambit to salvage the mission. Who was really working against them, and how do they turn it around?
